channel.title: Rustlang on Ivan Fraixedes' Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Rustlang on Ivan Fraixedes' Blog
channel.copyright: This works is license under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International license

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #13
item.description:
This week I’ve been half away, however I’ve collected several tweets and my choice has driven to centre the topic in Rust, Go and iojs.

As usual in my last post about “my 5 favourite weekly tweets”, I’ve got a blog post about Rust which help me to learn more about it in a passive way, in this case about Closures in Rust

Finding Closure in Rust http://t.co/M4m21WgY2i

— Rust Language (@rustlang) May 8, 2015

Now that I’m using go professionally a lot of doubts have bubble up with my colleagues in how to approach the implementation of several general parts of the system/application/serive, so I’ve seen this post which compares several approaches about net/context and http.Handler very interesting.

Go's net/context and http.Handler: https://t.co/vxKnSbsQLZ

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) May 9, 2015

Another post about Go got into my favourites ones, this time about something that I’d already known from a conference that CloudFlare’s team has been working on, a cryptography library to boost performance due the Go crypto package isn’t fast enough as existing solutions in other techs.

Go Crypto: Bridging The Performance Gap - https://t.co/jNEJjz6MQm

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) May 10, 2015

Another Rust blog post, in this case from its official blog, which teaches about traits and how they allows to abstract without an important overhead

Abstraction without overhead: traits in #rustlang http://t.co/yzDfHNCQaW

— Rust Language (@rustlang) May 11, 2015

And the last one it’s about the agreement between iojs and Node Foundation to unify forces to empower Node’s ecosystem and community

The @official_iojs just agreed to move into the Node Foundation and rename the "iojs" org on GitHub to "nodejs" http://t.co/W1cCakBgLy

— Rod Vagg.js (@rvagg) May 13, 2015

Have a nice spring weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #12
item.description:
Another “working” week is gone and as usual you can take a look here to my 5 favourite tweets of the week

I’ve got a nice post about Rust Iterators, don’t missed if you’ve started to be curious about this language that will be for production use in a few days after this post

A Journey into Iterators http://t.co/tuR0cIIG12

— Rust Language (@rustlang) May 2, 2015

Hacking is not my topic, however I’m always interested in news about it, so this tweet about Javascript-Based DDoS got my attention

An Introduction to JavaScript-Based DDoS: https://t.co/V6VU7agXOC

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) May 3, 2015

The amazing community behind iojs has made a big step to move forward releasing 2.0 version, they deserved to add this tweet here

io.js 2.0 Released: https://t.co/hcF5CxV1yV (V8 upgraded to 4.8, npm to 2.9.0 & more, see https://t.co/flwmTNnJvP )

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) May 5, 2015

The Linux distribution called Kali, has updated 3 packages related with Wireless Penetration Testing, so good news if you love to play around with wireless connections

Pixiewps, Reaver & Aircrack-ng - wireless penetration testing tool updates in Kali and what they mean for you - https://t.co/jgXn4t9nE1

— muts (@kalilinux) May 4, 2015

Dave Cheney has created a new build tool for Go, called gd which should solve the problem of a reproducible builds; one of thing that me and many other people miss in Go is an appropriated tool to manage dependencies so I’ll take a look to this totally different approach of others I’ve seen and tested, hence this tweet provided me this amazing discovery

Building Go projects with 'gb': https://t.co/f9igUiQOQP

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) May 8, 2015

Have a lovely weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #11
item.description:
This week I’m coming back with a 5 tech tweets, moreover I’ve realised that Rust got my heart.

Amazing blog post about how Rust makes the communication with C easy besides leveraging with its ownership system with zero-cost abstraction. It’s worthwhile to read it to see how it can also helps to create C bindings for other languages as Python, Ruby and Javascript

Rust once, run anywhere: http://t.co/AXF2uIFNav

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 24, 2015

Through this tweet, I found this awesome NodeJS module, massive-js, which allows to deal with PostgreSQL in a nice way

Massive 2.0: A Postgres-Centric Data Access Tool for Node.js - https://t.co/6TEBVxhBxS

— JavaScript Daily (@JavaScriptDaily) April 24, 2015

A nice video to watch another opinion about functional programming

Deconstructing Functional Programming http://t.co/nO9ITGfQat

— Dave Cheney (@davecheney) April 27, 2015

A nice post about writing some code in Ruby using the tooling.

"Rust is improving on many traditional systems languages by encouraging a good package ecosystem from the beginning" http://t.co/8S1OEsw7YQ

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 27, 2015

And the last one is this awesome research paper which tells the implementation of a Unix Kernel with Ruby; they haven’t got the same performance than C, but they are overall happy with the work.

Reenix: Implementing a Unix-Like Operating System in Rust http://t.co/ypLvs5aveq

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 27, 2015

Have a good weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #10
item.description:
This week, my first favourite tweet is not about tech, rare? where I have a few other interests as well, however don’t worry, the next four are tech, so go ahead

Starting a business from scratch isn’t probably the easiest thing to do, probably the opposite, so knowing from the experience of others is very valuable

Starting a business from Scratch - hints and tips from @dan_jenkins after he founded @nimbleapeltd http://t.co/Uz9JG1Zhom

— Nimble Ape Ltd (@nimbleapeltd) April 17, 2015

Rust is getting my attention, so learning language features through blog post is quite passive to get into it

Mixing, matching, mutation and moves http://t.co/YFE1702wiD

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 17, 2015

I love Internet, so I love the Web, reading about the new browser features is always in my todo list

Chrome Stable (42) now with Push Notifications and Add to Home screen for all users. http://t.co/tw2A9dt7uJ pic.twitter.com/prEOJmC0uQ

— Chrome Developers (@ChromiumDev) April 20, 2015

I’ve been very happy of the new features that npm release, with the amount of package that it hosts, it was something that so many people were expecting, and happy they’ve got solve a hard problem

solving npm’s hard problem: naming packages http://t.co/126RkmZaef pic.twitter.com/voENvgIiA7

— npmbot (@npmjs) April 21, 2015

I think that this series of blog posts about “Writing a Lexer and Parser in Go” will very good, because they teach golang based in a very familiar topic about computer science besides that the example is simple, so easy to step into, and based in a Rob Pike presentation.

Writing a Lexer and Parser in Go - Part 1: http://t.co/EvhFBi6L2X

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) April 20, 2015

Have a crazy weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #9
item.description:
This week security, programming languages and Linux, go ahead to see what tweets I’ve chosen.

New security issue on Apple OS X 10.10.2, so if you use OSX 10.10 and yo haven’t updated, you should do ASAP because you’re already late.

#rootpipe Full disclosure of a privilege escalation to root in Apple OS X 10.10.2 (and earlier): https://t.co/mT6EvSYqWh

— Emil Kvarnhammar (@emilkvarnhammar) April 9, 2015

A blog post about Rust and concurrency has been released, after it’s in beta and it’ll be for production soon, it’s worth to read what feature the language has to implement some interesting patterns

Fearless Concurrency with Rust http://t.co/RzmGXuPAcD

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 10, 2015

I wasn’t aware of the Elementary Linux distribution, I don’t have any plan to use but I’ve like the concept of it

Elementary Linux released: 32bit http://t.co/6I07CZ6IIe 64bit http://t.co/pewrEkzjWE More https://t.co/lV7wdkrXlZ pic.twitter.com/5dwrDqIC6Q

— nixCraft (@nixcraft) April 11, 2015

Indoctrinate people to Linux is always good, and making it free remove all the barriers, so I support this cause and I’ve decided to share it here; furthermore the author is the creator of one of the distributions that I used quite a bit in the past, Gentoo.

Please RT. The best "Linux Fundamentals" course, by Daniel Robbins the creator of Gentoo Linux http://t.co/uQMmw2w7yQ No registration needed

— nixCraft (@nixcraft) April 11, 2015

I’ve really found very interesting about a blog post written by @ploeh, because lastly I’ve got in the same thoughts about having too many features which some of them are redundant in programming languages, frameworks, etc., rather than help in long term, they annoy.

Less is more: removing language features to leave nowhere for bugs to hide. http://t.co/6q3MHSkAe9 by @ploeh

— Tim Ruffles (@timruffles) April 14, 2015

Have a wonderful weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #8
item.description:
Another short week is almost done and here my 5 favourite tweets of the last 7 days which appeared in my timeline.

The moment arrived, after several years under development, Rust beta has been released and the first stable release has been scheduled for 15th of May, so AWESOME

Rust 1.0-beta is here!! http://t.co/8HTh1jrL69

— Rust Language (@rustlang) April 3, 2015

Very nice web resource to see what ES6 brings and how these new features differ from its predecessor ES5

nice hands-on resource for learning ES6: http://t.co/VB5WcTT4LR

— Ilya Grigorik (@igrigorik) April 4, 2015

I’ve go quite old great blog post about Open Source and how delaying to make it open affects, at least when it’s created by a governmental organisation

another great post by @kfogel: Be Open [Source] From Day One http://t.co/8hTihlTZNo

— Aidan Feldman (@aidanfeldman) April 8, 2015

A friend of mine tweeted about [Ghost](https://ghost.org/) blog post where they’ve made public all their revenues metrics, I’m very thrilled how some companies, as Buffer 1 2, and Baremetrics 1 2 3, are moving toward the transparency

$411,000 in ARR and @TryGhost just made all of their revenue metrics completely public: http://t.co/SGH5CFmeQE

— BROLΛПDO (@orliesaurus) April 9, 2015

Javascript is moving to take over the stablished Java and .NET enterprise stacks, we’ll see what will happen

JavaScript will lead a massive shift in enterprise development http://t.co/07XVzu9LgK

— Paul Krill (@pjkrill) April 9, 2015

Have a great weekend!

item.title: My 5 favourite tweets of the week #2
item.description:
This week I’ve been delighted of

Rust lang released a new version 1.0.0-alpha.2, it’s the language that I want to take to write some lines of code, however until that moment, I’m following the evolution and reading some tips that I collect from the community

Rust 1.0.0.alpha.2 http://t.co/S72HcYo6t3 SO FREAKING EXCITING! #rustlang

— Community Opener (@flaper87) February 20, 2015

I’ve got the chance to have a read to an amazing technical blog post about how to reduce Go allocations which drove me to read other two posts in golang blog which allowed me to learn more about the internals

Reducing Allocations in Go Code: http://t.co/ODHknUsNU3

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) February 22, 2015

I’ve discovered a nice so nice open source service implemented in Go, Gogs, a self-hosted Git service

Gogs: A Self-Hosted Git Service Written in Go - http://t.co/juisUGaCPN (Think GitHub-lite.)

— Go Newsletter (@golangweekly) February 23, 2015

I could know the tradeoffs of why a big corporate, as Walmart, decide to run open source software, reading the first post of a new series

Why we run an open source program - Walmart Labs http://t.co/Ihy9dN2YLs via HN

— nixCraft (@nixcraft) February 25, 2015

iojs released a new version 1.4.1 with delightful promise/process, streams with TLS, new event for http, v8, libuv & npmjs update; the community behind this NodeJS fork never ceases to amaze us with their open governance model

io.js v1.4.1 is out @ https://t.co/MKaxWFkxcN incl new "unhandledRejection" & "rejectionHandled" events and more: https://t.co/9LPbP6VQxu

— Rod Vagg.js (@rvagg) February 26, 2015

I hope that you could enjoy with some of them as I did.

Have a lovely geeky weekend.
